questions.text: Which of the following intraoperative findings raise suspicion for bladder injury during laparoscopic hysterectomy? Select best 2 answers.

answerOptions.text: B) Clear fluid pooling in the operative field
C) Difficulty in identifying anterior vaginal wall during bladder flap development.
D) Air bubble in the bladder dome

questions.explanation: Hematuria and clear pooling fluid can signal cystotomy. Difficulty identifying the anterior vaginal wall is not specific. Small air bubbles at the bladder dome during cystoscopy can be normal.

questions.text: Which 3 pathophysiologic mechanisms can precipitate cardiac arrest during laparoscopy? Select 3 best answers.

answerOptions.text: B) High flow rate of insufflation in Trendelenburg
C) Sudden hypotension due to vasovagal reflex
D) Veress needle entry into bladder

questions.explanation: CO₂ embolism, vagal reflex from peritoneal stretch, and tension pneumothorax are established causes of arrest during laparoscopy.

questions.text: Which of the following are appropriate initial management steps for suspected major vascular injury during laparoscopy in gynecologic surgery? Select best 3 answers.

answerOptions.text: B) Laparotomy and direct pressure to bleeding site
E) Hold pressure with laparoscopic instrument.

questions.explanation: Deflate, convert for exposure and direct pressure, and mobilize vascular help. Trendelenburg is for gas embolism; laparoscopic pressure alone is a temporizing measure.
questions.submodule: Module 10.8 – Vascular Injury and Hemodynamic Instability

questions.text: Which 2 findings are most predictive of delayed intestinal thermal injury after electrosurgery? Select 2 best answers.

answerOptions.text: A) Air bubbles from bowel noted during surgery
B) Progressive leukocytosis and fever 3–5 days post-op
D) Blue-gray discoloration of small bowel serosa

questions.explanation: Delayed thermal injury often presents days later with fever and leukocytosis; subtle serosal discoloration intraop can herald evolving necrosis.

questions.text: Which of the following statements about bowel injury after laparoscopy are incorrect? Select all that are incorrect.

answerOptions.text: A) All injuries are diagnosed intraoperatively
B) Most thermal injuries present within 24 hours
C) Post-op peritonitis may indicate delayed perforation
D) Laparoscopic repair is appropriate for select injuries
E) Enterotomy should be irrigated and closed in two layers

questions.explanation: Many injuries (especially thermal) are recognized post-op and may present days later; peritonitis can signal delayed perforation. Selected injuries are amenable to laparoscopic repair with appropriate closure.

questions.text: A 2 cm cystotomy occurs during laparoscopic hysterectomy. Which of the following is least appropriate for management?

answerOptions.text: E) Visualization of the ureteral orifices

questions.explanation: A 2 cm injury needs repair, bladder fill test, catheter drainage, and cystoscopic evaluation—not observation.

questions.text: What is the most common site of bladder injury during laparoscopic hysterectomy?
questions.explanation: Injuries most often occur at the bladder dome/posterior wall during bladder flap development.

questions.text: Which factor is most associated with unrecognized bladder injuries?
questions.explanation: Routine cystoscopy detects occult injuries; omission increases missed-injury risk.

questions.text: After abdominal insufflation begins with a Veress needle, the patient becomes asystolic. What is the first step in management?
questions.explanation: Immediately stop insufflation and release pneumoperitoneum for suspected gas embolism/vagal event.

questions.text: What is the most likely cause of sudden asystole during initial insufflation with the Veress needle?
questions.explanation: Peritoneal stretch can trigger a vagal reflex leading to profound bradycardia/asystole.

questions.text: Which of the following increases the risk for subcutaneous emphysema during laparoscopy?
questions.explanation: High insufflation pressures and prolonged cases increase extraperitoneal gas tracking risk.

questions.text: During Veress needle entry, the abdomen fails to insufflate and blood is seen. The patient is hypotensive. What is the next step?
questions.explanation: Hemodynamic instability with suspected major vascular injury warrants emergent laparotomy.
questions.submodule: Module 10.8 – Vascular Injury and Hemodynamic Instability

questions.text: What is the definition of a massive transfusion?
questions.explanation: ≥10 units of PRBCs in 24 hours is the standard definition triggering MTP.
questions.submodule: Module 10.8 – Vascular Injury and Hemodynamic Instability

questions.text: In postoperative hypovolemic shock, which is not a typical physical exam finding?
questions.explanation: Hypovolemia typically causes tachycardia; bradycardia is atypical.
questions.submodule: Module 10.8 – Vascular Injury and Hemodynamic Instability

questions.text: What is the most appropriate management of a suspected thermal bowel injury?
questions.explanation: Thermal injury depth is unpredictable; resection with margins is safest.

questions.text: Which of the following mechanisms is least likely to cause thermal bowel injury?

answerOptions.text: A) Direct application of monopolar energy
D) Bipolar energy used on omentum

questions.explanation: Properly used bipolar energy has limited lateral spread and is least likely to injure bowel.

questions.text: What is the most common time frame for clinical presentation of thermal bowel injury?
questions.explanation: Delayed necrosis commonly declares itself between POD 4–10.

questions.text: Which of the following symptoms most strongly suggests occult bowel injury postoperatively?

answerOptions.text: C) Worsening abdominal pain and distension

questions.explanation: Progressive pain/distension several days post-op is worrisome for bowel injury.

questions.text: What is the imaging modality of choice for suspected postoperative bowel injury?

answerOptions.text: D) CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast

questions.explanation: Contrast-enhanced CT is most sensitive/specific for postoperative bowel injury.

questions.text: Which of the following is the most appropriate management when bowel injury is suspected post-op despite a negative CT scan?
questions.explanation: If clinical suspicion remains high, operative exploration is warranted despite negative imaging.
